items.passage: Age-graded schooling is the practice of grouping students into class levels by birth year, forming the backbone of most national education systems. In the earliest classrooms, a single teacher taught children of different ages together in one room, moving between beginners and older pupils in the same session. Lessons proceeded at whatever pace suited the instructor, since no shared standard fixed what a child should already know at a given age. This informal arrangement grew unworkable as populations expanded and reformers pushed for universal attendance. Nineteenth-century officials grouped students loosely by age to manage the crowding, yet lessons still relied on rote recitation, and pupils who fell behind simply repeated the same material without extra support. [■] Teachers lacked shared textbooks, so a student transferring schools might face material studied years earlier. The breakthrough came when reformers such as Horace Mann established graded classrooms built around a fixed curriculum for each year. [■] Every student in a grade then encountered the same material in the same order, letting teachers plan around a predictable starting point. [■] Standardized promotion between grades let administrators track progress consistently across a district. [■] However, critics note that grouping strictly by age can still leave slower or faster learners poorly matched to their class pace.
paragraphs: Age-graded schooling is the practice of grouping students into class levels by birth year, forming the backbone of most national education systems. In the earliest classrooms, a single teacher taught children of different ages together in one room, moving between beginners and older pupils in the same session. Lessons proceeded at whatever pace suited the instructor, since no shared standard fixed what a child should already know at a given age.

This informal arrangement grew unworkable as populations expanded and reformers pushed for universal attendance. Nineteenth-century officials grouped students loosely by age to manage the crowding, yet lessons still relied on rote recitation, and pupils who fell behind simply repeated the same material without extra support. [■] Teachers lacked shared textbooks, so a student transferring schools might face material studied years earlier.

The breakthrough came when reformers such as Horace Mann established graded classrooms built around a fixed curriculum for each year. [■] Every student in a grade then encountered the same material in the same order, letting teachers plan around a predictable starting point. [■] Standardized promotion between grades let administrators track progress consistently across a district. [■] However, critics note that grouping strictly by age can still leave slower or faster learners poorly matched to their class pace.

questions.stem: The word "unworkable" in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to

options.A: affordable
options.B: flexible
options.C: unmanageable
options.D: temporary

questions.explanation: The passage says the informal arrangement "grew unworkable as populations expanded," meaning it became unmanageable.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 1, how were early one-room classrooms organized?

options.A: Students were divided into separate rooms based on their individual reading levels.
options.B: One teacher guided pupils of many different ages within a single room.
options.C: Each age group met separately with the teacher on alternating days of the week.
options.D: Wealthy families hired private tutors instead of sending children to school.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 states that "a single teacher taught children of different ages together in one room," which option B paraphrases.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 2 about the education children received during this period?

options.A: It was completely standardized across every region.
options.B: It focused mainly on preparing students for standardized national examinations each spring.
options.C: It varied significantly depending on which school or teacher a child had.
options.D: It was generally better organized and more consistent than the schooling that existed before it.

questions.explanation: Because teachers lacked shared textbooks and a transferring student might face material studied years earlier, the content of instruction clearly varied by school.

questions.stem: Why does the author mention Horace Mann in paragraph 3?

options.A: To argue that Mann's reforms ultimately failed to improve schools.
options.B: To compare tutoring methods used in different countries.
options.C: To explain how nineteenth-century attendance laws were enforced.
options.D: To credit a specific reformer with introducing graded classrooms.

questions.explanation: The passage credits reformers "such as Horace Mann" with establishing graded classrooms built around a fixed curriculum, illustrating who drove the breakthrough.

questions.stem: There are four locations [■] in the passage that indicate where the following sentence could be added. **'And because promotion depended entirely on a teacher's personal judgment, two children of the same age might advance at very different rates for reasons unrelated to ability.'** Where would the sentence best fit? Select a location to add the sentence to the passage.

options.A: Slot 1
options.B: Slot 2
options.C: Slot 3
options.D: Slot 4

questions.explanation: The sentence elaborates on why nineteenth-century promotion was inconsistent, fitting right after pupils who fell behind simply repeated material, at slot 1, and before the mention that teachers lacked shared textbooks.

items.passage: Metamorphic rock is rock whose original mineral structure has been transformed by intense heat and pressure without the material fully melting. This transformation occurs deep within the crust, where conditions differ sharply from those at the surface where the parent rock first formed. The resulting rock retains some trace of its origin, known as its protolith, even after its texture and mineral content change completely. These transformations fall into two broad categories that differ mainly in scale and cause. [■] Contact metamorphism affects only the rock immediately surrounding a body of molten magma, baking it much as an oven alters dough placed near its wall. [■] Regional metamorphism instead affects enormous volumes of rock across an entire mountain belt, driven by the sustained pressure of colliding tectonic plates rather than by nearby heat alone. Geologists use the minerals present in a sample to distinguish these two processes and estimate the depth and temperature at which it formed. [■] Certain minerals, such as garnet, crystallize only within narrow pressure and temperature ranges, so their presence pinpoints the conditions of formation with useful precision. [■] However, overlapping mineral assemblages can sometimes form under more than one combination of pressure and temperature, so identification from minerals alone may not always yield a single unambiguous answer.
paragraphs: Metamorphic rock is rock whose original mineral structure has been transformed by intense heat and pressure without the material fully melting. This transformation occurs deep within the crust, where conditions differ sharply from those at the surface where the parent rock first formed. The resulting rock retains some trace of its origin, known as its protolith, even after its texture and mineral content change completely.

These transformations fall into two broad categories that differ mainly in scale and cause. [■] Contact metamorphism affects only the rock immediately surrounding a body of molten magma, baking it much as an oven alters dough placed near its wall. [■] Regional metamorphism instead affects enormous volumes of rock across an entire mountain belt, driven by the sustained pressure of colliding tectonic plates rather than by nearby heat alone.

Geologists use the minerals present in a sample to distinguish these two processes and estimate the depth and temperature at which it formed. [■] Certain minerals, such as garnet, crystallize only within narrow pressure and temperature ranges, so their presence pinpoints the conditions of formation with useful precision. [■] However, overlapping mineral assemblages can sometimes form under more than one combination of pressure and temperature, so identification from minerals alone may not always yield a single unambiguous answer.

questions.stem: The word "pinpoints" in paragraph 3 is closest in meaning to

options.A: questions
options.B: estimates
options.C: conceals
options.D: identifies

questions.explanation: The passage states that certain minerals' presence "pinpoints the conditions of formation with useful precision," meaning it identifies them precisely.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, what mainly distinguishes contact metamorphism from regional metamorphism?

options.A: The chemical composition of the original protolith.
options.B: The scale of rock affected and the source of the transforming pressure.
options.C: The age of the mountain belt in which each occurs.
options.D: Whether or not garnet crystals happen to be present in the rock.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 says the two categories "differ mainly in scale and cause," contrasting a small zone heated by magma with an enormous volume driven by plate collision.

questions.stem: According to the passage, all of the following are true EXCEPT:

options.A: Metamorphic rock forms without the parent material fully melting.
options.B: Contact metamorphism occurs near a body of molten magma.
options.C: Certain minerals form only within specific pressure and temperature ranges.
options.D: Mineral assemblages always point to exactly one combination of conditions.

questions.explanation: The passage states that "overlapping mineral assemblages can sometimes form under more than one combination of pressure and temperature," directly contradicting option D.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 1 about the appearance of metamorphic rock?

options.A: It can look very different from the rock it originally was.
options.B: It always looks completely identical to the rock it originally formed from.
options.C: It cannot be linked to its original rock type at all.
options.D: It forms only at or near the surface of the crust.

questions.explanation: Since the rock's "texture and mineral content change completely" while only retaining "some trace" of its protolith, its appearance can differ greatly from its original form.

questions.stem: There are four locations [■] in the passage that indicate where the following sentence could be added. **'And because each mineral is stable only under a specific combination of conditions, its presence acts like a natural recorder of a rock's history.'** Where would the sentence best fit? Select a location to add the sentence to the passage.

options.A: Slot 1
options.B: Slot 2
options.C: Slot 3
options.D: Slot 4

questions.explanation: The sentence explains why mineral presence reveals formation conditions, leading directly into the example of garnet at slot 3.

items.passage: A catalyst is a substance that speeds up a chemical reaction without being permanently consumed in the process. Unlike a reactant, a catalyst emerges from the reaction chemically unchanged and available to act again. It achieves this effect by providing an alternative pathway with a lower energy barrier, known as activation energy, that the reacting molecules must cross before they can transform into products. This lowered barrier works because the catalyst temporarily binds to the reacting molecules, holding them in an orientation that favors the reaction. [■] The molecules attach to specific sites on the catalyst's surface or structure, weakening the existing bonds that must break for the reaction to proceed. [■] Once the new bonds form, the resulting product separates from the catalyst, which is then free to bind another set of reactant molecules. This cycle of binding and release allows a small quantity of catalyst to process an enormous number of reactant molecules over time. [■] Industrial chemists exploit this efficiency to manufacture products such as fertilizers and plastics using far less energy than uncatalyzed reactions would require. [■] However, catalysts can lose their effectiveness when impurities occupy their active sites, a problem that gradually limits how long a given batch remains useful.
paragraphs: A catalyst is a substance that speeds up a chemical reaction without being permanently consumed in the process. Unlike a reactant, a catalyst emerges from the reaction chemically unchanged and available to act again. It achieves this effect by providing an alternative pathway with a lower energy barrier, known as activation energy, that the reacting molecules must cross before they can transform into products.

This lowered barrier works because the catalyst temporarily binds to the reacting molecules, holding them in an orientation that favors the reaction. [■] The molecules attach to specific sites on the catalyst's surface or structure, weakening the existing bonds that must break for the reaction to proceed. [■] Once the new bonds form, the resulting product separates from the catalyst, which is then free to bind another set of reactant molecules.

This cycle of binding and release allows a small quantity of catalyst to process an enormous number of reactant molecules over time. [■] Industrial chemists exploit this efficiency to manufacture products such as fertilizers and plastics using far less energy than uncatalyzed reactions would require. [■] However, catalysts can lose their effectiveness when impurities occupy their active sites, a problem that gradually limits how long a given batch remains useful.

questions.stem: The word "exploit" in paragraph 3 is closest in meaning to

options.A: ignore
options.B: reduce
options.C: measure
options.D: utilize

questions.explanation: The passage says industrial chemists "exploit this efficiency to manufacture products," meaning they utilize it for production.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 1, how does a catalyst differ from a reactant?

options.A: It reacts more slowly than a typical reactant does.
options.B: It stays chemically unaltered and can be reused in another reaction.
options.C: It increases the total amount of energy released by the reaction.
options.D: It combines permanently with the final product of the reaction.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 states that a catalyst "emerges from the reaction chemically unchanged and available to act again," unlike a reactant, which option B paraphrases.

questions.stem: What does "this efficiency" refer to in paragraph 3?

options.A: the activation energy required by a reaction
options.B: the manufacture of fertilizers and plastics
options.C: the specific binding sites located on a catalyst's surface
options.D: a small amount of catalyst processing many molecules

questions.explanation: The previous sentence explains that "a small quantity of catalyst" can "process an enormous number of reactant molecules over time," which is the efficiency referred to.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 2 about reactant molecules before they bind to a catalyst?

options.A: They cannot react with each other under any circumstances.
options.B: They are chemically identical to the catalyst that will act on them.
options.C: Breaking their existing bonds normally requires a relatively large amount of energy.
options.D: They permanently alter the chemical structure of the catalyst.

questions.explanation: Because the catalyst works by "weakening the existing bonds that must break," those bonds must ordinarily require more energy to break without the catalyst's help.

questions.stem: There are four locations [■] in the passage that indicate where the following sentence could be added. **'For example, an enzyme's active site is shaped to fit its target molecule much as a lock accepts a specific key.'** Where would the sentence best fit? Select a location to add the sentence to the passage.

options.A: Slot 1
options.B: Slot 2
options.C: Slot 3
options.D: Slot 4

questions.explanation: The enzyme example illustrates how binding favors a specific orientation, fitting right after that idea is introduced at slot 1 and before the description of molecules attaching to specific sites.

items.passage: Domestication is the process by which early farmers selectively bred wild plants and animals across generations to make them more useful for human purposes. Unlike wild varieties, domesticated organisms display traits that rarely persist in nature, such as larger seeds, softer husks, or a calmer temperament. These changes accumulated slowly, as farmers repeatedly saved seeds or bred animals that showed the most desirable traits. This gradual selection worked because farmers, whether deliberately or not, favored plants and animals easiest to grow or manage. Wild wheat scattered its seeds before harvest, offering farmers little to collect, so any plant whose seeds clung to the stalk until cut was more likely to be gathered and replanted. Over generations, this repeated, unconscious preference shifted the population toward the trait that suited harvesting, though no farmer bred for it directly. This shift toward high-yield, easily harvested varieties had consequences beyond individual farms. Reliable, concentrated harvests let communities store surplus food and remain settled year-round, rather than moving seasonally in search of wild resources. Denser settlements followed, along with the specialized labor and larger populations that later characterized early cities. However, dependence on a narrow set of crops left these communities more vulnerable to failed harvests than foragers with a broader diet had been.
paragraphs: Domestication is the process by which early farmers selectively bred wild plants and animals across generations to make them more useful for human purposes. Unlike wild varieties, domesticated organisms display traits that rarely persist in nature, such as larger seeds, softer husks, or a calmer temperament. These changes accumulated slowly, as farmers repeatedly saved seeds or bred animals that showed the most desirable traits.

This gradual selection worked because farmers, whether deliberately or not, favored plants and animals easiest to grow or manage. Wild wheat scattered its seeds before harvest, offering farmers little to collect, so any plant whose seeds clung to the stalk until cut was more likely to be gathered and replanted. Over generations, this repeated, unconscious preference shifted the population toward the trait that suited harvesting, though no farmer bred for it directly.

This shift toward high-yield, easily harvested varieties had consequences beyond individual farms. Reliable, concentrated harvests let communities store surplus food and remain settled year-round, rather than moving seasonally in search of wild resources. Denser settlements followed, along with the specialized labor and larger populations that later characterized early cities. However, dependence on a narrow set of crops left these communities more vulnerable to failed harvests than foragers with a broader diet had been.

questions.stem: The word "persist" in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to

options.A: continue
options.B: increase
options.C: disappear
options.D: compete

questions.explanation: The passage describes traits that "rarely persist in nature," meaning they rarely continue to exist in wild populations.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, why were seeds that clung to the stalk more likely to be replanted?

options.A: Because they produced noticeably more grain per individual plant.
options.B: Because they remained available for farmers to gather at harvest time.
options.C: Because they ripened somewhat earlier in the season than the scattering varieties.
options.D: Because farmers deliberately bred them for that specific trait.

questions.explanation: The passage explains that scattering seeds "offered farmers little to collect," while clinging seeds were "more likely to be gathered and replanted," which option B paraphrases.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 2 about the process of domestication?

options.A: It always required a carefully planned and thoroughly deliberate breeding program.
options.B: It happened within a single growing season for most crops.
options.C: It could occur without farmers intentionally selecting for a specific trait.
options.D: It affected only plant species and never applied to animals.

questions.explanation: Because the shift toward the harvestable trait occurred "though no farmer bred for it directly," domestication could result from unconscious rather than deliberate selection.

questions.stem: Why does the author mention wild wheat in paragraph 2?

options.A: To argue that wheat was the very first crop ever domesticated.
options.B: To show that wild wheat is no longer eaten by anyone.
options.C: To compare wheat farming practices across different regions.
options.D: To give a concrete example of unconscious trait selection.

questions.explanation: The wild wheat example illustrates concretely how seeds that clung to the stalk were more likely to be gathered, demonstrating the unconscious selection process described in the paragraph.

questions.stem: According to the passage, all of the following are true EXCEPT:

options.A: Domesticated organisms often display traits that are rare in wild populations.
options.B: Domestication could occur through unconscious rather than deliberate selection.
options.C: Settled agricultural communities eventually supported larger populations.
options.D: Communities relying on domesticated crops became less vulnerable to failed harvests.

questions.explanation: The passage states that dependence on a narrow set of crops "left these communities more vulnerable to failed harvests than foragers," directly contradicting option D.

items.passage: Object permanence is the understanding that objects continue to exist even when they cannot be seen, heard, or touched. Researchers in the early twentieth century assumed infants lacked this understanding, since young babies rarely searched for a toy hidden from view. Their failure to retrieve a hidden object was taken as evidence that, for an infant, an unseen thing had ceased to exist. This assumption was refined, though not overturned, by Jean Piaget, who proposed that object permanence develops gradually through a fixed sequence of stages. Piaget based his timeline mainly on whether infants physically searched for hidden objects, a motor skill that also depends on memory and coordination developing separately. Because his method conflated understanding with the ability to act on it, his timeline placed true object permanence later than it actually occurs. This limitation was addressed decades later through looking-time methods that no longer required infants to reach for anything. By measuring how long babies stared at physically impossible events, scientists found that infants only months old already expected hidden objects to persist. These findings shifted the field toward viewing early cognition as more sophisticated than reaching-based tests had suggested. However, researchers still disagree how much of this competence is present from birth rather than acquired later.
paragraphs: Object permanence is the understanding that objects continue to exist even when they cannot be seen, heard, or touched. Researchers in the early twentieth century assumed infants lacked this understanding, since young babies rarely searched for a toy hidden from view. Their failure to retrieve a hidden object was taken as evidence that, for an infant, an unseen thing had ceased to exist.

This assumption was refined, though not overturned, by Jean Piaget, who proposed that object permanence develops gradually through a fixed sequence of stages. Piaget based his timeline mainly on whether infants physically searched for hidden objects, a motor skill that also depends on memory and coordination developing separately. Because his method conflated understanding with the ability to act on it, his timeline placed true object permanence later than it actually occurs.

This limitation was addressed decades later through looking-time methods that no longer required infants to reach for anything. By measuring how long babies stared at physically impossible events, scientists found that infants only months old already expected hidden objects to persist. These findings shifted the field toward viewing early cognition as more sophisticated than reaching-based tests had suggested. However, researchers still disagree how much of this competence is present from birth rather than acquired later.

questions.stem: What is the passage mainly about?

options.A: Why Piaget's original stage theory of cognitive development remains the single most accurate model available.
options.B: How looking-time experiments are conducted in modern psychology laboratories.
options.C: How scientific understanding of infants' grasp of object permanence has changed as new methods emerged.
options.D: Why infants are physically unable to search for hidden objects during their first year.

questions.explanation: The passage traces beliefs about infant object permanence from early assumptions through Piaget's stage theory to modern looking-time findings, matching the full scope of option C.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, what problem existed with Piaget's method of studying object permanence?

options.A: It ignored infants entirely and instead studied only older children.
options.B: It relied on parental reports rather than direct observation of behavior.
options.C: It assumed object permanence developed instantly rather than gradually over time.
options.D: It depended on a physical ability that matures separately from cognitive understanding.

questions.explanation: The passage explains that Piaget's timeline relied on a "motor skill that also depends on memory and coordination developing separately," conflating a physical ability with understanding itself.

questions.stem: How does paragraph 3 relate to paragraph 2?

options.A: It repeats Piaget's original findings using different terminology.
options.B: It argues that Piaget's theory was mistaken from the very beginning.
options.C: It questions whether infants understand hidden objects at all.
options.D: It presents a later approach that corrected a limitation described earlier.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 identifies a limitation in Piaget's reaching-based method, and paragraph 3 opens by noting that "this limitation was addressed decades later" through looking-time methods.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 3 about infants' cognitive abilities?

options.A: They fully understand object permanence from the very moment of birth.
options.B: Their competence may have been underestimated by earlier reaching-based tests.
options.C: They perform noticeably worse on looking-time tasks than on reaching tasks.
options.D: They lose the ability to track hidden objects as they grow older.

questions.explanation: Because looking-time methods revealed understanding that reaching-based tests had missed, the passage implies infants' true competence had been underestimated by the earlier approach.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 1, how did early twentieth-century researchers interpret infants' failure to search for hidden toys?

options.A: They concluded that infants believed unseen objects no longer existed.
options.B: They concluded that infants were physically unable to move their arms.
options.C: They concluded that infants simply preferred toys that stayed visible.
options.D: They concluded that limited memory, not belief, was the main factor.

questions.explanation: The passage states this failure "was taken as evidence that, for an infant, an unseen thing had ceased to exist," which option A paraphrases.
